funding since 2019. It's the general fund where we had to make all of those reductions the past
couple of years. So on the legislative front, as we know, we're all in a legislative session. Last
week the Senate followed the House in passing Senate Bill 2, which is the voucher bill, with a

[06:00–06:30]
billion dollar price tag. This is the largest initial voucher bill in the country, and it is moved

recently, the Texas House of Representatives passed House Bill 2, its public education funding bill.
The House promotes or as promotes proposes $8.1 billion for public education, including a $5.1
billion for increased investment in public education.

[06:30–07:00]

system. Currently it's not fully funded, and $750 million for teacher performance pay. The Senate,
on their side, does not include an increase in the basic allotment, which is something that we have
advocated for.

[07:00–07:30]

the passage of the voucher bill, there is speculation that the attitude for funding public schools

budge on a compromise with

[46:00–46:30]

[47:00–47:30]

discretion of the board president not to exceed four minutes. We will do four minutes today. Because
individual rights are protected by various laws and board policies, complaints regarding specific
employees or students should be directed to the administration. If a person makes complaints about a
specific employee or a student during public comment,

[47:30–48:00]
Comment, the board president or vice president has the discretion to move the board into closed
session to hear such complaints. If anyone makes a complaint about a specific employee by name or by
job title, the board may hold the public comment until the end of public comments and hear all such
complaints individually in closed session. Under the law, the board cannot deliberate, discuss, or
make a decision on any subject that is not on the agenda. So, as a general matter, the board does
not respond to questions at public comment. Specific factual information or review of existing
policy, however,

[48:00–48:30]

Hi, good evening. My name is Nicole Hobson and I live at 403 Electra Drive. I'm speaking about the
second reading of the GT policy.

[48:30–49:00]
Policy item 7C, specifically about the section that gives control and accountability of GT spendings
to the superintendent. I realize that many of you don't see this as a concern, as this is the

we implored each of you to intervene on our behalf. We repeatedly heard that this was an
administration decision and thus not a board decision, which is why the changes to spiral would
never be brought to a board vote. Over the past year, parents have been not only raising significant
funds to keep the spiral program intact, but also adding

[49:00–49:30]
But also advocating with legislators and making trips to Austin to try to raise funds for gifted and
talented education. We are doing everything in our power to keep this program that is so impactful
to so many. We are taking these actions with no guarantee that if additional funding for GT services
is provided by the state, that it would in fact go to preserving spiral. The change in GT policy is
concerning as while we are actively working to raise money, the board is cementing the position that
GT spending is not in your purview. While we are coordinating with districts across the state to
push for GT funding, we are at risk that this funding, if it comes in,

[49:30–50:00]
will be used how the administration sees fit, which may or may not include funding the 40-year
program that serves nearly 1200 students on a weekly basis. Dr. Blaine has repeatedly shared that
